reviewerResults.persona: Small startup CTO with low to medium AWS experience. Cares most about time-to-production and shipping quickly without getting trapped in AWS complexity.
strengths: Strong opening paragraph — self-contained, defines what the resource is, why it matters, and what it's built on, all within the target word count. Works well as an AI-extractable answer.

The 'When should I use a worker service?' and alternatives table are concrete and opinionated. Each row names a specific situation and links to the better option, helping the reader make a fast decision.
The basic example pairs Stacktape config with a realistic SQS-polling application code snippet — this is exactly what a startup CTO needs to see to understand the pattern end-to-end.

Appropriately omits sections that don't apply to worker services (load balancing, CDN, CORS, custom domains, gradual deployments, firewall) while keeping the structure parallel to the web service page for navigational consistency.

The health checks section includes the <Info> callout explaining why worker services only have internal health checks (no load balancer). This cross-resource comparison helps readers who are also looking at web-service docs.
Pricing context is grounded in source data and correctly noted upfront. The cost table for Fargate sizes is practical and matches the pricing source file.
The Connecting to other resources example realistically pairs a WorkerService with an SqsQueue resource, which is the most common worker pattern — good signal-to-noise.
problems: The connectTo example references 'myDatabase' in the connectTo array but never defines it in the config. This will confuse readers who expect a complete, copyable example. Either define a database resource or remove the reference.

No mention of `stopTimeout` anywhere in the page. For worker services doing background processing, graceful shutdown is critical — the default of 2 seconds is very short and likely insufficient for most queue consumers. The reader needs to know this exists and that they probably need to increase it.

The Persistent Storage section is thin compared to other sections — it shows how to mount EFS but doesn't explain when you'd want persistent storage on a worker (e.g., local file processing, ML model caching) or the cost/performance tradeoffs of EFS.
mandatoryFixes: Add a mention of `stopTimeout` (default: 2 seconds, range: 2–120) either in its own short subsection or as a Tip/Warning near the health checks or basic example section. For background workers processing jobs, 2 seconds is almost always too short — the reader needs to know to increase this to avoid dropped work during deployments.
Fix the connectTo example: either add a relational database resource definition alongside the SqsQueue, or remove 'myDatabase' from the connectTo array so the example is self-contained and copyable.
optionalImprovements: Consider adding a short 'Graceful shutdown' subsection or paragraph explaining the SIGTERM → SIGKILL lifecycle, the stopTimeout property, and the pattern for draining in-flight work before shutdown. This is the #1 operational concern for queue-consuming workers.

The Persistent Storage section would benefit from a one-line 'when to use' framing (e.g., 'Use EFS when your worker needs to read/write files that persist across container restarts or are shared between instances') and a cost note (~$0.30/GB-month for standard EFS).

The Scaling section could mention that for SQS-based workers, scaling on CPU alone may not be ideal — a worker polling an empty queue has low CPU. Consider noting that custom CloudWatch metrics or queue depth-based scaling may be more appropriate for queue consumers, or link to a relevant page if one exists.

In the basic example's application code, the SQS polling loop has no error handling or graceful shutdown hook. Adding a brief comment like '// In production, handle SIGTERM to finish in-flight messages' would reinforce the stopTimeout guidance without bloating the example.

Consider using question-format headings for a few key sections (e.g., 'How do I scale a worker service?' instead of 'Scaling') to improve SEO discoverability, matching the style guide's preference for question-form H2/H3 headings 'where natural'.

reviewerResults.persona: SMB engineering manager with medium to high AWS knowledge. Cares about long-term maintainability, team clarity, operational fit, technically correct Stacktape class-based config examples, and valid MDX component usage.
strengths: Strong opening structure with a clear definition, pricing context, and early use/not-use guidance.
Uses TypeScript class-based Stacktape config examples inside <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/> and avoids forbidden YAML config patterns.
Covers the main operational levers this audience needs: compute sizing, scaling, connectTo, NAT for static outbound IP, health checks, logging, remote sessions, and EFS mounts.
The comparison table in the decision section is practical and helps distinguish worker services from web services, private services, Lambda functions, and batch jobs.
problems: Several technical claims are not grounded in the provided source files: worker-service zero-downtime deployments, Lambda 15-minute timeout, EC2 weekly refresh for patching, Fargate valid CPU/memory combinations, health check defaults, and CloudWatch retention details beyond what is shown in SimpleServiceContainer.
The basic example application reads STP_JOBS_URL but the paired Stacktape config does not define an SQS queue or connect the worker to it, so the example is not copyable as written.
The first CodeBlock examples use label: 'stacktape.ts' instead of the required visible TypeScript tab label from the supplied docs rules.
The page repeats several claims from the web-service example without re-grounding them for worker-service, especially around compute, scaling defaults, logging, and remote debugging.

The worker-service page does not clearly explain the important operational tradeoff for queue consumers: Stacktape does not attach event triggers to a worker service; the application process must poll or otherwise run its own loop.

Some claims are broader than the source supports, such as 'Stacktape manages scaling, health checks, and zero-downtime deployments' in the intro. The source supports scaling and internal health checks, but not the deployment behavior stated here.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or narrow unsupported claims unless they can be grounded in authoritative source files: zero-downtime deployments, Lambda timeout, EC2 weekly refresh, Fargate CPU/memory valid combinations, health check defaults, and log retention wording.
Make the basic example internally consistent: either define and connect an SQS queue in the config or change the application code so it does not depend on STP_JOBS_URL.

Change Stacktape config CodeBlock tab labels to the required visible TypeScript label, or align with the project’s validated component convention if the repository explicitly standardizes on stacktape.ts.
optionalImprovements: Add a short section explaining how worker services receive work: they are always-running containers with no public endpoint, so queue polling, stream clients, cron-like loops, or external clients must be implemented in the container code.
Add one sentence near scaling that CPU/memory-based scaling may not track queue backlog directly, if supported by source or validated elsewhere. That would help managers reason about operational fit.
Consider moving persistent storage earlier or adding a brief 'skip EFS unless you need shared durable files' tradeoff, since EFS changes cost and operational behavior.
Link CLI command mentions such as stacktape logs and stacktape container:session to their CLI reference pages if routes exist in the docs source.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer with little AWS or DevOps experience. Needs jargon kept under control and wants examples that are easy to follow.
strengths: Excellent 'When should I use a worker service?' section with a clear decision table covering five alternatives — matches the example page pattern and gives the reader a fast answer.

The 'Connecting to other resources' example is notably stronger than a bare connectTo list: it defines the SqsQueue alongside the worker in the same config, showing the full pattern a real team would use.
Health checks section adds a well-placed <Info> callout that explicitly contrasts with web service (no load balancer health check), helping readers who are comparing the two resource types.

Persistent storage section is a smart addition that the example web-service page doesn't have — EFS mounts are more relevant for workers (shared state across instances, surviving restarts) and the example is realistic and copyable.
Pricing context is accurate, sourced from the distilled pricing file, and includes the critical caveat that idle tasks still bill — important for a resource that cannot scale to zero.
All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with TypeScript class-based resources; no style violations detected.
problems: The basic example has a critical disconnect: the app code references `process.env.STP_JOBS_URL` (which requires `connectTo: ['jobs']` and a defined SqsQueue resource), but the config only defines the worker with packaging and resources — no queue, no connectTo. A beginner copying this example verbatim gets an undefined env var. The web-service example page avoids this by using a fully self-contained HTTP server.

The basic example's worker code uses a bare `while (true)` polling loop with no error handling or backoff. A beginner deploying this will get a container that crashes on the first SQS error and restarts in a loop. A minimal try/catch or a note about production error handling would prevent a common foot-gun.

The `stopTimeout` property (default 2 seconds, range 2–120) from `SimpleServiceContainer` is not mentioned anywhere. For a worker service processing long-running jobs, graceful shutdown is critical — a 2-second default may silently drop in-progress work. This deserves at least a one-line mention.

The first paragraph says 'built on AWS ECS Fargate' without a brief gloss. The target audience (little AWS/DevOps experience) may not know what Fargate is. The pricing line partially addresses this ('Fargate task'), but a parenthetical like 'a serverless container platform' on first mention would help.
mandatoryFixes: Fix the basic example so the config and app code are consistent. Either: (a) make the basic config self-contained by adding an SqsQueue and `connectTo: ['jobs']` (similar to the later 'Connecting to other resources' example), or (b) change the basic worker code to a simpler self-contained pattern (e.g., a periodic polling loop that doesn't depend on external resources) and keep the SQS pattern for the connectTo section.

Add a brief mention of `stopTimeout` (default 2 seconds) near the health checks or scaling section. For workers processing long jobs, the default 2-second shutdown window is a significant gotcha. Example: 'Set `stopTimeout` (default: 2 seconds, max: 120) to give your worker time to finish in-progress work before the container is replaced.'
optionalImprovements: Add a brief parenthetical on first mention of ECS Fargate for the target audience, e.g., 'built on AWS ECS Fargate (a serverless container platform)' — one clause, no extra section needed.

Add minimal error handling to the basic example's polling loop (a try/catch around processMessage with a short backoff on error) so beginners don't deploy a crash-loop. Even a comment like `// Add error handling for production` would help.

Consider adding a one-liner about verifying the worker is running after deploy, e.g., 'After deploying, run `stacktape logs --resourceName worker` to confirm your worker started and is processing messages.' This bridges the gap between 'deploy' and 'verify it works'.

The 'Scaling' section could briefly mention that for queue-based workers, CPU/memory-based scaling may not be the best signal — a queue depth alarm or custom metric might be more appropriate. Even a short note with a link to alarms would help.

Use a question-format heading for 'How do I connect to other resources?' and 'How do I configure health checks?' to improve SEO discoverability, matching the style guide's preference for question-form H2/H3 headings.

issues.reason: SES is covered by `connectTo` via the `aws:ses` macro. The `iamRoleStatements` JSDoc lists Rekognition, Textract, and Bedrock as examples — not SES. Listing SES here will mislead readers into writing IAM statements when `connectTo: ['aws:ses']` already handles it.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: "For permissions not covered by `connectTo` (e.g., AWS Rekognition, Textract, Bedrock), use `iamRoleStatements` to add raw IAM policy statements."

issues.statement: A worker service is an always-on container with no public endpoint, built on AWS ECS Fargate.
issues.reason: The opening paragraph states the worker service is 'built on AWS ECS Fargate', but it can also run on EC2 instance types. The `resources` property JSDoc explicitly lists two compute engines: Fargate (default) and EC2. The later 'Resources and compute' section correctly describes both, but the opening paragraph will be extracted by AI engines as the canonical description and currently omits the EC2 option.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: 'A worker service is an always-on container with no public endpoint, running on AWS ECS (Fargate by default, EC2 optional).' or 'built on AWS ECS Fargate (with an EC2 option for cost optimization).'

evidence.quote:
Two compute engines:

   * - **EC2**: Specify `instanceTypes` for more control and potentially lower cost.

issues.statement: The `architecture` option is only available with Fargate, not EC2.
issues.reason: The source says the architecture property is 'Ignored when using `instanceTypes`', not 'only available with Fargate'. EC2 instance architecture is determined by the instance type itself (e.g., c6g.large is ARM). Saying it's 'not available' on EC2 is misleading — it's simply unnecessary since instance types inherently define the architecture.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: 'The `architecture` property applies to Fargate only. When using EC2 `instanceTypes`, the architecture is determined by the instance type itself (e.g., `c6g.large` for ARM).'

This is a minor omission, not an error.

issues.reason: The 'refreshed weekly for patching' claim does appear in the EC2 instanceTypes JSDoc, so it is technically sourced. However, this detail is buried and readers may misunderstand what 'refreshed' means operationally. Low severity since the source does contain this statement.

positiveFindings: Pricing table is accurately derived from source data: all five rows match the distilled pricing (vCPU rate + memory rate). For example, 0.25 vCPU ($7.29) + 512 MB ($1.60) = $8.89 ≈ ~$9.
Scaling defaults (minInstances: 1, maxInstances: 1, CPU threshold: 80%, memory threshold: 80%) all match the source type definitions exactly.
Health check defaults (interval 30s, timeout 5s, 3 retries) are accurately stated and match the ContainerHealthCheck type @default annotations.
The `logging.retentionDays` property name is correct per ContainerWorkloadContainerLogging, and the 90-day default matches the source.

connectTo environment variable naming convention (STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]) and the specific variables for SQS (ARN, NAME, URL) and relational databases (CONNECTION_STRING, HOST, PORT) all match the ResourceAccessProps JSDoc.
NAT Gateway cost (~$32/month), availability zone range (1-3), and default (2) all match the NatSettings type definition.
Side container types ('run-on-init' and 'always-running') and their behavioral descriptions match the ServiceHelperContainer type and JSDoc.
All CodeBlock examples use the correct <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> format for Stacktape config, with no hand-authored YAML tabs or fenced TypeScript blocks for config.
The opening paragraph is well-crafted for SEO/AEO: self-contained, under 60 words, defines what the resource is and its primary use cases.
The 'when to use / when not to use' comparison table correctly maps alternatives to relevant resource pages.
The stopTimeout property constraint (2-120 seconds, default 2) in the source is not misrepresented in the draft — it's correctly left to the API reference component.
The usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT property and its behavior description match the SimpleServiceContainer source accurately.
verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-grounded overall. Three medium-severity issues: (1) SES is incorrectly listed as needing `iamRoleStatements` when it is already covered by the `connectTo` macro `aws:ses`; (2) the opening paragraph claims the worker service is 'built on AWS ECS Fargate' without mentioning the EC2 compute option, which the resource explicitly supports; (3) the `architecture` property is described as 'only available with Fargate, not EC2' when the source says it is 'ignored when using instanceTypes' — a meaningful distinction since EC2 architecture is determined by instance type. All pricing data, scaling defaults, health check defaults, logging retention, connectTo variable names, NAT configuration, and side container behavior are accurately sourced. CodeBlock format follows the style guide correctly throughout.

The draft is mostly aligned with the provided worker-service and shared container workload types, but it overstates several behaviors that are not grounded in the provided source and includes a few likely-invalid or unsupported example details. The largest problems are around deployment behavior, scaling defaults, health-check defaults, EC2 maintenance behavior, static outbound IP wording, EFS encryption wording, and unsupported links/CLI claims.

Question-format H2 headings ('How do I configure CPU and memory?', 'How does auto-scaling work?') match real search queries and make the page easy to scan.
The health check section includes a practical heartbeat-file pattern that gives users a working mental model for container health monitoring in a worker context (no HTTP endpoint to probe).

issues.statement: Adjust retention with the `logging.retentionDays` property — available values range from 1 day to 10 years.
issues.reason: The property name `retentionDays` and the range are correct (1 to 3653 days ≈ 10 years), but the allowed values are a discrete set (1, 3, 5, 7, 14, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 365, 400, 545, 731, 1827, 3653), not a continuous range. Saying 'range from 1 day to 10 years' may mislead readers into thinking any number of days is accepted.
issues.suggestedFix: Say 'available retention periods include 1, 3, 5, 7, 14, 30, 60, 90 days and up to 10 years' or 'select from predefined retention periods between 1 day and 10 years'.

issues.statement: Scaling policy defaults: scale out when average CPU or memory exceeds 80%, scale in when utilization drops below the target.
issues.reason: The 80% default is correct per source, but the draft implies both CPU and memory are evaluated by default. In reality, both `keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder` and `keepAvgMemoryUtilizationUnder` are optional. If only one is set (as in the example which only sets `keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder: 70`), only that metric triggers scaling. The draft's earlier example only sets CPU, but this sentence implies both are active by default.
issues.suggestedFix: Clarify that CPU and memory scaling targets are independent — each only activates when explicitly set in `scalingPolicy`, or both default to 80% when `scalingPolicy` is configured without specifying either.

issues.statement: `maxInstances` must be greater than or equal to `minInstances`.
issues.reason: This constraint is logical and almost certainly enforced at runtime, but the source type definition (`ContainerWorkloadScaling`) does not document this constraint. It only says both default to 1. The claim is likely correct but not grounded in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the statement (it's a sensible constraint) but no change needed — just noting it's not sourced from the type definition.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-sourced overall. Pricing computations, resource defaults, type properties, and behavioral claims are almost entirely grounded in the source type definitions and pricing data. The most notable issue is the inclusion of a FAQ section despite explicit instructions prohibiting it — the FAQ content is redundant with the main body. Minor issues include slightly imprecise wording around logging retention (discrete allowed values presented as a continuous range), the `architecture` property path not being fully qualified, and a subtle implication that both CPU and memory scaling targets are active by default. All core factual claims — pricing, defaults for stopTimeout/scaling/health checks, CPU values, side container behavior, NAT Gateway costs, and connectTo patterns — verified correctly against the TypeScript type definitions.

The draft is mostly grounded in the provided worker-service and shared container source, but it contains several unsupported or over-broad claims around scaling behavior, health-check behavior, EFS encryption, CLI command names, and MDX/style requirements. The biggest factual risks are claims that exceed the WorkerService/SimpleServiceContainer type comments and pricing sources.

The source only says multiple containers can mount the same volume; it does not specifically say multiple worker service instances can share files through the mount.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the exact sourced scope: “Persistent EFS volumes keep data when containers are replaced. Multiple containers can mount the same volume, and data is encrypted in transit.” Avoid “between container instances” unless supported by EFS or runtime source.

evidence.quote: Data stored in EFS volumes persists even when containers are replaced.
Multiple containers can mount the same volume. All data is encrypted in transit.

issues.statement: “Use persistent storage when your worker needs to cache large files (ML models, media assets), share state between container instances, or maintain data that survives container replacements.”
issues.reason: Caching large files and sharing state between worker instances are application recommendations not present in the provided source. The source only describes persistent EFS volumes shared across containers and restarts, with data persisting when containers are replaced.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the grounded use case: “Use `volumeMounts` when data must survive container replacement or be available to multiple mounted containers.”

evidence.quote: #### Persistent EFS volumes shared across containers and restarts.
Data stored in EFS volumes persists even when containers are replaced.

issues.statement: “Adjust retention with the `logging.retentionDays` property — available values range from 1 day to 10 years.”
issues.reason: The provided source supports that logging exists, that stdout/stderr goes to CloudWatch, and that logs are retained for 90 days, but it does not include the `ContainerWorkloadContainerLogging` definition, the `retentionDays` property, or the allowed retention values.

evidence.quote: Container output (`stdout`/`stderr`) is automatically sent to CloudWatch and retained for 90 days.
logging?: ContainerWorkloadContainerLogging;

suggestions: Rename noun-phrase headings to question format for consistency and search alignment: 'Basic example' → 'How do I deploy a basic worker service?', 'Graceful shutdown' → 'How does graceful shutdown work?', 'Connecting to other resources' → 'How do I connect a worker to databases and queues?', 'Side containers' → 'How do I run side containers alongside my worker?'

Mention 'Stacktape' in the opening answer paragraph so LLM extractions carry brand context. E.g., 'A Stacktape worker service is an always-on container…' — without this, an extracted snippet loses attribution.

The 'Where are worker service logs stored?' section is thin (two sentences). Expand it into a self-contained paragraph that mentions CloudWatch log group naming, how to query logs via CLI ('stacktape logs --resourceName worker'), and the available retentionDays values — this is a common search query that deserves a snippet-worthy answer.

Add a 'How do I run a worker service on EC2 instead of Fargate?' subsection or FAQ entry. The page mentions EC2 as an option in the opening paragraph and the resources section but never shows how to configure it — this is a likely search query users would have.

The 'How do I debug a running container?' section is a single short paragraph. Consider adding a brief code example showing the CLI command ('stacktape container:session --resourceName worker --stage prod') and mentioning what tools are available inside the session — this would make the section extractable as a standalone answer.

reviewerResults.persona: Small startup CTO with low to medium AWS experience. Cares most about time-to-production and shipping quickly without getting trapped in AWS complexity.
strengths: The SQS queue consumer example in the 'connecting to databases and queues' section is excellent — it gives a realistic, end-to-end pattern (config + application code) that a startup CTO could copy and adapt immediately.
The graceful shutdown section with the explicit warning about the 2-second default is high-value practical guidance that prevents a common production foot-gun. Most docs skip this.

Strong 'when to use / when not to use' section with a clear comparison table. The alternatives are correctly scoped — batch-job for finite tasks, Lambda for sporadic work, private-service for internal HTTP.

Pricing is surfaced immediately in the first paragraph, including the ARM discount and the critical caveat that Fargate cannot scale to zero. This helps the CTO make a cost-informed decision before reading further.
Question-format H2 headings ('How do I configure CPU and memory?', 'How does graceful shutdown work?') match what users actually search for and make scanning fast.
The EFS volume mounts section is a good inclusion — it answers the natural 'how do I persist data?' question that background workers frequently need, and the example is practical.
problems: The basic example's application code is a generic while-loop with setTimeout. For a worker service page, the first example the reader sees should demonstrate the most common pattern — polling a queue or processing a stream — rather than a placeholder loop. The SQS example later is excellent but appears too far down the page.

The scaling section states 'Setting minInstances to 0 is not supported' which is correct for Fargate, but could be more precise: it is an ECS/Fargate constraint, not a Stacktape limitation. A reader might wonder if Stacktape is artificially limiting them.

The environment variables section is thin — it shows static string values but doesn't mention secrets integration ($Secret directive) or dynamic values, which are common needs for background workers connecting to external services.
mandatoryFixes: In the scaling section, clarify that the min-1 constraint comes from Fargate, not Stacktape: e.g., 'Fargate requires at least one running container — setting minInstances to 0 is not supported.' This prevents the reader from thinking Stacktape is the bottleneck.
optionalImprovements: Consider moving the SQS queue consumer example higher — either make it the basic example, or add a brief note after the basic example saying 'For a realistic queue-processing pattern, see Connecting to databases and queues below.' The SQS pattern is the most common worker use case and should be prominent.

In the environment variables section, add a one-line mention of the $Secret directive for injecting secrets (API keys, tokens) — this is a very common need for background workers calling external APIs and the reader shouldn't have to hunt for it.

The scaling section could benefit from a brief note on how to right-size scaling policies for workers vs. web services: worker CPU often stays low while memory grows (queue backlog), so keepAvgMemoryUtilizationUnder may be more useful than CPU-based scaling for queue consumers.

Add a brief mention of dev mode behavior for worker services — whether `stacktape dev` runs the worker locally or connects to the deployed version, since the target audience will want to develop locally.

The Fargate cost table is identical to the web-service page. Consider adding a brief note about EC2 cost comparison for long-running workers — e.g., 'For workers that run 24/7, EC2 instances can be 40-60% cheaper than Fargate at the cost of more operational complexity.' This helps the startup CTO make a cost-informed compute engine choice.

reviewerResults.persona: SMB engineering manager with medium to high AWS knowledge. Cares about long-term maintainability, team clarity, operational fit, technically correct Stacktape class-based config examples, and valid MDX component usage.
strengths: Strong early decision framing: the page quickly distinguishes worker services from web services, private services, Lambda functions, and batch jobs.
Stacktape configuration examples use class-based `defineConfig` inside `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/>` and avoid the forbidden YAML/plain-object patterns.
The page covers the important 80% for this resource: packaging, compute sizing, scaling, shutdown, resource access, health checks, logs, remote sessions, NAT, side containers, and EFS volumes.
The SQS consumer example is practical and connects directly to a common worker-service use case.
problems: The opening sentence says worker service is "built on AWS ECS Fargate", but the source says `resources` supports Fargate by default and EC2 as another compute engine. This overstates Fargate as the only underpinning.

Several claims are broader than the provided worker-service source supports, including `stacktape deploy` behavior, managed ECR repository upload behavior for this resource page, and detailed alternative-resource descriptions such as batch jobs being finite tasks.

The auto-scaling section says `minInstances: 0` is not supported and that Fargate always runs at least one container. The source states Fargate cannot scale to zero and idle tasks bill, but does not directly document the `minInstances` constraint in the provided files.

The graceful shutdown section gives a `stopTimeout` range of 2-120 and default 2, which is grounded, but the statement about `SIGTERM` then `SIGKILL` should be phrased as Stacktape/container behavior from the source rather than expanded into application guidance without a small handler example.

The page may be too long for the audience relative to the API reference. Some sections, especially environment variables, side containers, EFS, logging, and remote debugging, are useful but could be tighter or linked out when they repeat common container workload concepts.
optionalImprovements: Change the first paragraph to say the worker runs as an always-on container workload, with Fargate as the default compute engine, rather than saying it is built on Fargate unconditionally.
Narrow unsupported statements or remove them unless additional source files are provided, especially the `stacktape deploy` result, managed ECR upload wording, and batch-job alternative description.

For scaling, keep the sourced point that Fargate cannot scale to zero and idle tasks bill; avoid asserting an explicit `minInstances: 0` validation rule unless the schema or validator source confirms it.
Add a short `SIGTERM` handler application-code snippet or reduce the graceful-shutdown prose to the sourced `stopTimeout` behavior.
Consider trimming lower-priority sections and relying on `<ApiReference definitionName="WorkerServiceProps" />` for exhaustive option coverage.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer with little AWS or DevOps experience. Needs jargon kept under control and wants examples that are easy to follow.
strengths: Strong opening paragraph that works as a self-contained answer: defines what a worker service is, names the underlying AWS service, lists use cases, and states the key constraint (no public endpoint) — all in ~50 words.

Excellent 'When should I use a worker service?' section with a clear comparison table that points directly to better alternatives for each mismatched scenario. This is exactly what a developer with little AWS experience needs to make a decision.
The graceful shutdown section with the Warning about the 2-second default is high-signal practical guidance that prevents a common production mistake. The SIGTERM handling advice is actionable.

The SQS consumer example in 'How do I connect to databases and queues?' is realistic and copyable — it shows both the Stacktape config and the full application code, including long-polling and message deletion. A reader can go from zero to working queue consumer with this.
Question-format H2 headings ('How does auto-scaling work?', 'How do I get a static outbound IP?') match what developers actually search for, improving both scannability and SEO/AEO discoverability.
Good coverage of EFS volume mounts — a practical need for workers that process files or maintain state across container replacements, and not something obvious from the example web-service page.
Correctly omits sections that don't apply (load balancing, CDN, CORS, custom domains, gradual deployments, firewall) rather than padding with 'not applicable' boilerplate.
problems: The CodeBlock tab label uses 'TypeScript' while the human-reviewed example page consistently uses 'stacktape.ts'. The style guide text says 'TypeScript' but the example page — which was reviewed and accepted — uses 'stacktape.ts'. This creates a section-level inconsistency if both pages ship together.

The logging section lists specific CloudWatch retention values (1, 3, 5, 7, 14, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 365, 3653) that are not present in the provided source files. The source only says 'retained for 90 days' in the JSDoc. While these are real CloudWatch values, the page should not assert specifics not grounded in the provided source.

The CPU/memory section states 'Available CPU values: 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16' but the provided source files do not list these values. The example page omits such a list. Including unsourced specifics risks being wrong if Stacktape constrains the set differently.

The 'Environment variables' section is thin — it only shows static string values. It doesn't mention that values can be directive references ($Secret, $ResourceParam) as documented in the EnvironmentVar type's JSDoc. For a developer who needs to pass secrets, this omission could send them down the wrong path.
mandatoryFixes: Align CodeBlock tab labels with the example page: change all `label: 'TypeScript'` to `label: 'stacktape.ts'` for section-level consistency with the human-reviewed web-service page.

Remove the enumerated retention values list ('1, 3, 5, 7, 14, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 365, and up to 3653 days') from the logging section. Replace with wording grounded in source: 'retained for 90 days by default. Adjust retention with the logging.retentionDays property.' This matches the example page's treatment and avoids asserting unsourced specifics.
optionalImprovements: Remove the 'Available CPU values: 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16' sentence from the CPU/memory section. The cost table already covers the common sizes, and the full set belongs in the API reference. If kept, verify the list against source.

In the 'Environment variables' section, add a brief note that values can reference secrets or other resource parameters using directives — e.g., 'Values can also reference secrets ($Secret) or other resource parameters ($ResourceParam). See [directives](/configuration/directives/) for details.' This helps the target audience discover a critical capability.

In the graceful shutdown section, add a one-line concrete example of SIGTERM handling (e.g., `process.on('SIGTERM', () => { ... })`) since the target audience has little DevOps experience and may not know how to listen for signals in Node.js.

Consider adding a brief note in the scaling section that scaling policy targets default to 80 when `scalingPolicy` is configured but not explicitly set — the example page includes this detail and it helps users understand implicit behavior.

In the 'How do I detect and replace unhealthy containers?' section, briefly explain what 'CMD-SHELL' means in the health check command array, since this Docker concept may be unfamiliar to the target audience.

issues.statement: A Stacktape worker service is an always-on container with no public endpoint, built on AWS ECS Fargate.
issues.reason: The opening sentence states the worker service is 'built on AWS ECS Fargate', but it also supports EC2 as a compute engine. The draft corrects this later in the CPU/memory section, but the opening paragraph — which AI engines extract verbatim — presents Fargate as the only option. This matters for SEO/AEO since the opening is supposed to be a self-contained answer.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: 'A Stacktape worker service is an always-on container with no public endpoint, running on AWS ECS (Fargate by default, or EC2).' or 'built on AWS ECS Fargate (default) or EC2.'

evidence.quote:
Two compute engines:
   * - **Fargate** (default): Serverless — just specify `cpu` and `memory`.
   * - **EC2**: Specify `instanceTypes` for more control and potentially lower cost.
instanceTypes?: string[];

issues.statement: Adjust retention with the logging.retentionDays property — supported values include 1, 3, 5, 7, 14, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 365, and up to 3653 days (~10 years).
issues.reason: The draft lists supported retention values but omits 400, 545, 731, and 1827 from the actual union type. It also says 'up to 3653 days' implying a continuous range, when the actual type is a discrete union. The incomplete list could mislead users who want one of the omitted values.

issues.statement: Fargate does not support scaling to zero — at least one container is always running and billed.
issues.reason: The sentence attributes the constraint to Fargate ('Fargate does not support scaling to zero'), but AWS Fargate itself does support scaling ECS services to zero desired tasks. The constraint is at the Stacktape level (minInstances minimum is 1). The pricing source does say 'Fargate cannot scale to zero' but this is Stacktape-contextual. Attributing it to Fargate itself is technically inaccurate and could confuse users with Fargate experience.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: 'Stacktape does not support setting minInstances to 0 — at least one container is always running and billed.' or 'Worker services cannot scale to zero — at least one container is always running and billed.'

evidence.quote: Minimum running instances. Set to 0 is not supported — minimum is 1.

issues.statement: Use startPeriodSeconds to give the container a grace period (0–300 seconds) during startup before health check failures count.
issues.reason: The source type defines startPeriodSeconds as optional with the JSDoc 'Grace period (seconds) before counting failures. Gives the container time to start (0-300).' but does not specify a default value. The draft's 'Defaults: interval 30s, timeout 5s, 3 retries before marking unhealthy' section omits mentioning the startPeriodSeconds default, which is fine, but a reader might assume it defaults to 0. The source is silent on the default.
issues.suggestedFix: Explicitly note that startPeriodSeconds has no default (or defaults to 0 if that can be verified), so the grace period must be set explicitly if needed.

evidence.quote: Grace period (seconds) before counting failures. Gives the container time to start (0-300).

issues.statement: Only set targets activate scaling — if you only set CPU, memory utilization is not monitored.

The behavior depends on whether an omitted property with a @default still activates — the types don't clarify this runtime behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Verify runtime behavior: does omitting keepAvgMemoryUtilizationUnder when scalingPolicy is set still use the default of 80? If yes, clarify that both targets are active by default. If no, clarify that only explicitly set targets are active despite having type-level defaults.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-grounded overall. Most factual claims — pricing, property names, defaults, type shapes, and behavioral descriptions — match the source files. Two medium issues: (1) the opening paragraph says 'built on AWS ECS Fargate' but EC2 is also a supported compute engine, and (2) the logging retention values list is incomplete (missing 400, 545, 731, 1827 from the actual union type). Three low issues relate to ambiguous attribution of the scale-to-zero constraint (Stacktape limitation, not Fargate), unclear startPeriodSeconds default, and uncertain runtime behavior of scaling policy defaults. No forbidden MDX patterns, no hand-authored YAML, and all component references are valid.

The draft is broadly consistent with the WorkerService type surface and avoids the major forbidden config-example patterns. The main factual problems are over-narrowing worker services to Fargate, missing pricing caveats, one unsupported outbound-IP default claim, and an overconfident scaling-policy default interpretation.

issues.statement: “A Stacktape worker service is an always-on container with no public endpoint, built on AWS ECS Fargate.”
issues.reason: Worker services inherit the simple service container resource model, where Fargate is the default compute engine but EC2 is also supported through `instanceTypes`. The first paragraph is designed for extraction, so saying it is built on Fargate overstates the default as the whole feature.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: “A Stacktape worker service is an always-on container with no public endpoint. By default it runs on Fargate, and it can use EC2 when you configure instance types.”

evidence.quote:
Two compute engines:
   * - **Fargate** (default): Serverless — just specify `cpu` and `memory`.
   * - **EC2**: Specify `instanceTypes` for more control and potentially lower cost.

issues.statement: “A minimal Fargate task (0.25 vCPU, 512 MB) costs ~$9/month” and the monthly cost table.
issues.reason: The pricing source requires these figures to be treated as us-east-1 ballpark list prices and says they exclude storage, I/O, and data transfer. The draft mentions us-east-1 in the table but not the exclusion caveat near the pricing context line.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short caveat near the top pricing line: “These are us-east-1 ballpark list prices for one on-demand task and exclude storage, I/O, and data transfer.”

evidence.quote: All prices are us-east-1 list price for an on-demand single instance/task and exclude storage, I/O, and data transfer. Use these as ballpark figures — quote them with "~" or "approximately" in docs.

issues.statement: “Only set targets activate scaling — if you only set CPU, memory utilization is not monitored.”
issues.reason: The type source documents both CPU and memory scaling targets as optional fields with `@default 80`. The draft states a specific omitted-target behavior that is not clearly supported by the type comments and may conflict with the documented defaults.
issues.suggestedFix: Write more narrowly unless runtime validators confirm the behavior: “`scalingPolicy` supports CPU and memory utilization targets. The source documents a default target of `80` for each target; set the targets explicitly when you want predictable scaling behavior.”

issues.statement: “By default, your container's outbound IP address changes with each deployment.”
issues.reason: The source supports the positive claim that `usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT` provides a static outbound IP via NAT Gateway, but it does not document the default outbound IP behavior or say it changes on each deployment.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the default-behavior claim. Use: “If third-party allowlists require a stable outbound IP, enable private subnets with NAT.”

suggestions: Rename 'Basic example' to a question-format heading like 'How do I deploy a worker service?' to match the pattern used by the other headings and align with how users actually search.
Rename 'Environment variables' to 'How do I set environment variables?' and 'Side containers' to 'How do I run side containers?' for consistency and better search query alignment.

Add a brief FAQ section at the bottom (before API reference) that consolidates 3-5 key questions with concise 1-2 sentence answers — e.g., 'Can a worker service scale to zero?' (No, Fargate always runs at least one container; use Lambda for scale-to-zero), 'How much does a worker service cost?' (From ~$9/month for 0.25 vCPU / 512 MB; ~20% less with ARM), 'Does a worker service have a public URL?' (No, use a web service for public endpoints). These are high-value Featured Snippet targets.

The logging section ('Where are worker service logs stored?') is a single dense paragraph mixing retention values, CLI commands, and the disable flag. Break it into a short intro paragraph plus a small code example showing `logging: { retentionDays: 30 }` — this makes the retention config extractable by AI engines and scannable by readers.

In the opening answer block, consider adding 'no public URL' alongside 'no public endpoint' since users search for both phrasings. Minor wording tweak: 'A Stacktape worker service is an always-on container with no public URL or endpoint...' captures both search variants.

The 'How do I debug a running container?' section is only two sentences. Expand slightly with a 2-3 line code example (`stacktape container:session --resourceName worker`) so the section is substantive enough for AI engines to extract as a standalone answer.
